coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble near-infrared AgInS2 quantum dots for Ca2+ detection and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Li, Jinhua
Cheng, Mingming
Li, Li
Yan, Ruhong
Yue, Juan
coredata.dc:description: Calcium ions (Ca2+) are key players in intracellular signaling as second messengers and play a pivotal role in various physiological processes. In this study, near-infrared water-soluble AgInS2 quantum dots (AIS QDs) for Ca2+ detection were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. The fluorescence quantum yield (PL QY) of the quantum dots was as high as 23.99 %. With low cytotoxicity and good fluorescence properties, as well as short reaction time, the ternary AIS QDs have excellent synthesis efficiency and quantum yield, which are advantageous for Ca2+ detection and bioimaging applications. The fluorescence quenching of the quantum dots showed a clear linear relationship with calcium ion concentration in the range of 0–250 μM (detection limit: 0.65 μM). Confocal imaging experiments demonstrate the excellent biofluorescence imaging capability of AIS QDs. By tuning the Ag/In molar ratio, AIS QDs can achieve fluorescence emission in the near-infrared wavelength band (620–700 nm), and the near-infrared fluorescence imaging has deeper tissue penetration, less tissue absorption and photodamage, and lower interference of spontaneous fluorescence, which further expands the potential of QDs for bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Fluorescent probes
Ca2+ detection
Cellular imaging
Biocompatibility

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2022YFC2403500 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82172077 , 22201298 and 62027825 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China ( BK20231207 ), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province ( JSSCTD202145 ), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2023SYHZ0037 ), the Projects of Chinese Academy of Sciences ( YJKYYQ20200038 and YJKYYQ20210032 ), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS ( 2022326 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM electron microscopy image of AIS QDs. (b) HR-TEM electron microscopy images of AIS QDs. (c) Histogram of particle size distribution of AIS QDs with an average particle size of about 3.17 nm.(d) XRD spectra of AIS QDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of AIS QDs (blue line), TGA (red line), and EGTA (black line). (f) XPS spectra of AIS QDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, emission and excitation spectra of AIS QDs. (b) Emission spectra of AIS QDs under different excitation light. (c) Total fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram of AIS QDs.
(a) AIS QDs solution under 450 nm irradiation (left), original AIS QDs solution under room temperature condition (right). (b) Fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs under different Ag/In ratios.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of AIS QDs solutions containing different concentrations of calcium ions. (b) Linear plot of lg(F0/F) versus Ca2+ concentration (0–250 μM).

Confocal imaging of HeLa cells, (a-c) Bright field, fluorescence field and merged images of AIS QDs containing 500 μM Ca2+. (d-f) Bright field, fluorescence field and merged images of AIS QDs without added Ca2+.

Confocal imaging of GL261 cell line, (a-c) Bright field, fluorescence field and merged images of AIS QDs without added Ca2+. (d-f) Bright field, fluorescence field and merged images of AIS QDs containing 500 μM Ca2+.

(a-c) CLSM images of zebrafish under incubation with AIS QDs-containing cultures. (d-f) Zebrafish CLSM images under incubation with AIS QDs-containing and Ca2+ cultures. (g-i) CLSM images of zebrafish eggs incubated with AIS QDs-containing culture medium. the QDs concentration was 1 mg/mL−1 and the scale bar was 250 μm.

CLSM images of HeLa cells. (a) Merged images of AIS QDs, DAPI and brightfield. (b) Fluorescence image of AIS QDs incubation. (c) Fluorescence image of DAPI incubation. (d) Brightfield image of HeLa cells.
Schematic representation of the preparation process of AIS QDs and its application in Ca2+ detection and bioimaging.

head.ce:title: Water-soluble near-infrared AgInS2 quantum dots for Ca2+ detection and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Suzhou Hospital, Affiliated Hospital of Medical School, Nanjing University, Suzhou 215153, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Suzhou Hospital, Affiliated Hospital of Medical School, Nanjing University, Suzhou 215153, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Calcium ions (Ca2+) are key players in intracellular signaling as second messengers and play a pivotal role in various physiological processes. In this study, near-infrared water-soluble AgInS2 quantum dots (AIS QDs) for Ca2+ detection were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. The fluorescence quantum yield (PL QY) of the quantum dots was as high as 23.99 %. With low cytotoxicity and good fluorescence properties, as well as short reaction time, the ternary AIS QDs have excellent synthesis efficiency and quantum yield, which are advantageous for Ca2+ detection and bioimaging applications. The fluorescence quenching of the quantum dots showed a clear linear relationship with calcium ion concentration in the range of 0–250 μM (detection limit: 0.65 μM). Confocal imaging experiments demonstrate the excellent biofluorescence imaging capability of AIS QDs. By tuning the Ag/In molar ratio, AIS QDs can achieve fluorescence emission in the near-infrared wavelength band (620–700 nm), and the near-infrared fluorescence imaging has deeper tissue penetration, less tissue absorption and photodamage, and lower interference of spontaneous fluorescence, which further expands the potential of QDs for bioimaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Calcium ion is one of the most abundant metal ions in living organisms, 99 % of which are found in bones and teeth and the rest in blood and tissues. Calcium ions are indispensable metal ions for the body to carry out various physiological activities, and are closely related to blood coagulation, regulation of neuronal function, muscle contraction, and immunity of the organism [1–4]. In addition, calcium ions are involved in hormone secretion, normalizing intra and extracellular calcium levels [5]. Abnormal calcium levels can lead to life-threatening diseases and disorders. Fluctuations in calcium ion levels can lead to diseases such as cancer [6], cardiovascular disease [7], muscle disease [8], Alzheimer's disease [9], and hypoparathyroidism [10]. Because calcium ions are so closely related to human physiological activities, the detection of calcium ions in the body and the development of biological applications and research are of great practical importance.

Due to the important role of calcium ions in the human body, changes in their concentration and state can serve as important markers of changes in a variety of physiological activities, so changes in calcium ions are used as experimental indicators in more and more studies, especially in the fields of medicine and life sciences and other areas of application and research has received extensive attention from researchers [11,12]. In the past decades, calcium ion detection methods have been rapidly developed, including plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [13,14], ion chromatography [15], potentiometric method [16], microcantilever in situ detection [17], colorimetric method [18], atomic spectral absorption method [19] and other detection means have been rapidly developed, However, all of the above techniques generally suffer from the defects of low sensitivity, cumbersome sample preparation process, low detection efficiency, expensive instrumentation cost, and cannot guarantee the timeliness of detection [20], so they are not suitable for in vivo detection and biological applications.

With the advent of laser scanning confocal microscopy (CLSM) in the 1980s [21,22], the study of Ca2+ measurements in cells and tissues of living organisms have been significantly accelerated. Image processing using computers and laser excitation of fluorescent probes result in fluorescent images of the internal microstructure of cells or tissues for detecting Ca2+ concentration or observing changes in cell morphology. Compared with inherited detection methods, fluorescent probe detection of Ca2+ has received extensive attention from researchers due to its advantages of low cost, high sensitivity, and rapid detection [23,24]. Different types of fluorescent probes have far-reaching research significance in bioanalysis, virus analysis, cellular imaging, in vivo diagnosis, and antiviral research due to the different advantages of their material properties [25]. Among them, fluorescent probes with quantum dots as indicators have excellent performance and are widely used in bioimaging, medical diagnosis and treatment, and biomedical applications [26–28].

In contrast to fluorescent proteins and small molecule materials, semiconductor quantum dots are inorganic nanomaterials, which are nanoparticles with a size of less than 20 nm. Due to the quantum size-limited domain effect and surface effect unique to the nanoscale, quantum dots have very special properties in fluorescence and have outstanding contributions to biomedical research [29,30]. Among them, semiconductor quantum dot materials such as PbS, CdTe, and HgTe have excellent optoelectronic properties, but their advancement to biological applications has been hindered by the presence of heavy metal elements Pb, Cd, and Te [31–33]. In recent years, ternary I-III-VI quantum dot materials (CuInS2, CuInSe2, AgBiS2, AgInS2) have gradually attracted the attention of researchers because of their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility [34]. AgInS2 was identified as a better candidate with better physicochemical advantages over conventional binary quantum dots, including broad excitation spectrum and narrow emission spectrum, large Stokes shift, long fluorescence lifetime, good stability and biocompatibility. Moreover, the tuning of the emission spectrum from orange to near-infrared bands can be realized by changing the Ag/In ratio, which makes it more widely applicable [35,36]. Currently, the synthesis methods of AIS QDs are mainly organic and aqueous phase synthesis, of which the most typical organic phase is the thermal injection method. Chang's group reported an AIS QDs synthesized by thermal injection method with a quantum yield of 22 % for cellular imaging [37]. High fluorescence quality quantum dots can be obtained by organic-phase synthesis, but the complexity of synthesis conditions and poor biocompatibility have hindered research progress. Aqueous-phase synthesis can avoid the need to improve water solubility through ligand exchange or surface coating while ensuring biocompatibility and environmental stability, yielding higher-quality AIS QDs that are advantageous for biological applications. For example, Xue et al. prepared water-soluble AIS QDs for the detection of Pb2+ by an aqueous one-pot method using glutathione (GSH) as a stabilizer [38]. Other researchers also developed microwave-assisted synthesis of core–shell structured AIS/ZnS quantum dots can be used for cell fluorescence imaging and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [39]. The above studies surface that AIS QDs have the ability to detect and analyze biomolecules and metal ions, and have abundant potential for development.

In this study, water-soluble AIS QDs emitting near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence were synthesized through a straightforward one-step hydrothermal synthesis method, employing ethylene glycol diethyl ether diamine tetraacetic acid (EGTA) and mercaptoacetic acid (TGA) for modification (Scheme 1 ). The synthesized quantum dots exhibit outstanding fluorescence properties and biocompatibility, demonstrating non-excitation dependent fluorescence emission and remarkable stability. Utilizing EGTA as the ligand for modification and capping, the inherent calcium-chelating property was leveraged for sensitive detection of Ca2+, ensuring exceptional performance. A distinct linear fluorescence quenching phenomenon was observed within the range of 0–250 µM, attributed to the aggregation of the QDs induced by the complexation of EGTA with Ca2+ and the light-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, leading to fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, HeLa cells and GL261 cells were chosen for cellular imaging, revealing bright red fluorescence of QDs in co-localization analysis. This observation was extended to conduct in vivo zebrafish fluorescence imaging. The experimental findings underscored the potential of AIS QDs for real-time detection of Ca2+ both in vivo and ex vivo, showcasing their promising applications in cellular and biological fluorescence imaging.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), indium nitrate hydrate (In(NO3)·xH2O), sodium sulfide hydrate (Na2S·9H2O), thioglycolic acid (TGA) were purchased from Adamas Reagent (Shanghai) Co. Ethylene glycol diethyl ether diamine tetraacetic acid (EGTA), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium iodide (NaI), chromium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cr(NO3)3), mercuric sulfate (HgSO4), ferric chloride (FeCl3), ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), and anhydrous copper sulfate (CuSO4), Zinc acetate anhydrous (C4H6O4Zn), Manganese chloride anhydrous (MnCl2), Calcium chloride (CaCl2), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co. In addition, anhydrous ethanol, fetal bovine serum (FBS), RPMI 1640 medium, phosphate buffered saline solution (PBS), penicillin–streptomycin solution, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and 4 % paraformaldehyde were purchased from Thermo Fisher.The reagents used were analytically pure and could be used directly without the need for secondary operations. Zebrafish embryos and larvae were supplied by Nanjing EzeRinka Biotechnology Co.

Electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained using a FEI Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscope. The samples were analyzed using a Bruker D8 advanced x-ray diffractometer to obtain x-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) and an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) to obtain x-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). Spectral analysis was performed using a Nicolet™ iS50 FTIR spectrometer to obtain Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a PE750 spectrometer, fluorescence spectra were captured using an F97 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer, fluorescence quantum yields were measured using an FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer, fluorescence images of cells and zebrafish embryos and juveniles were acquired by a Leica TCS-SP5 confocal microscope system.

AIS QDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.06 mmol of AgNO3 and 0.24 mmol of In(NO3)·xH2O were dissolved in 35 ml of ultrapure water and stirred until the solution became transparent. Subsequently, 0.5 mmol of EGTA and 50 μL of mercaptoacetic acid were added dropwise, resulting in the solution turning milky white with noticeable precipitation. The pH was adjusted to 8 using 0.1 M NaOH solution, and after 20 min of ultrasonic dispersion, a clear transparent solution was obtained. Next, 0.3 mmol of Na2S·9H2O was added and sonicated for ten minutes. The solution was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel autoclave and heat-treated at 100 °C for 60 min. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min to remove any unreacted impurities. Excess ethanol was added to precipitate the product, which was subsequently re-dissolved in deionized water (repeated three times at 10,000 r/min) and stored under light-proof conditions for further experiments.

After first freeze-drying the QDs solution using a vacuum lyophilizer, the solid powder was collected for the next experiments. A 0.1 M masterbatch of calcium ions was prepared using CaCl2, and then diluted to different concentrations (concentration range 0–250 μM) of Ca2+ solution was added to 1 mg/mL−1 of AIS QDs solution, which was mixed thoroughly and then the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs solution was measured using fluorescence spectrophotometer under the excitation light of 470 nm, and the change of the fluorescence intensity was recorded for the different concentrations of calcium ions. The fluorescence intensity was linearly fitted to establish a linear relationship between the change in fluorescence intensity and Ca2+ concentration.

In order to test the specificity of the quantum dot solution for Ca2+, different ion solutions were selected for interference tests under fixed concentration conditions, and the fluorescence changes under the coexistence of interfering ions and calcium ions were tested at the same time. The above experiments were carried out at room temperature.

To assess the cytotoxicity of QDs, cell activity assays were performed using the WST-1 Cytotoxicity Assay Kit. Hela cells were first inoculated in 96-well cell culture plates at an incubation density of 1x104 /well, and the medium used was penicillin–streptomycin solution, 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 90 % RPMI 1640 medium. After incubation for 24 h at 37 °C (5 % CO2) in an incubator, the cells were well adhered to the wall and replaced with fresh medium, different concentrations of AIS QDs were dissolved in PBS solution and added to the 96-well plate at 10 μL per well, and then incubated for 24 h. The medium was discarded and the cells were washed three times with PBS solution, and WST-1 cytotoxicity assay reagent was added to each well, and the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, the absorbance of all wells at 450 nm was recorded using an enzyme marker, and the cell viability was calculated using the formula（1）.
Survival rate
=
OD
Treatment group
-
OD Blank
OD
Control group
-
OD Blank
×
100\%

Firstly, to evaluate the imaging ability of quantum dots in cells, we used HeLa cells and GL261 cells for co-incubation experiments, respectively. HeLa cells were inoculated into petri dishes for cellular confocal imaging (5x103/dish), and incubated for 24 hrs in a 37 °C (5 % CO2) incubator, and then the original medium was replaced with fresh RPMI 1640 medium (DMEM for the incubation of GL261 cells) for another 12 hrs, and 100 μg/mL−1 AIS QDs solution was added. The original medium was replaced with fresh RPMI 1640 medium (GL261 cells were incubated with DMEM medium), and 100 μg/mL−1 of AIS QDs solution was added for another 12 h of incubation. After removing the QDs-containing medium, the dishes were washed three times with PBS solution, and the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde as a fixative for 15 min, and then added with the cellular nucleic dye Dapi staining for 20 min, and the dishes were washed three times with PBS solution after the incubation was finished. Finally, the cells were imaged using a Leica TCS-SP5 confocal microscope.

In order to examine the imaging ability of AIS QDs in living animals, we chose zebrafish for bioimaging. Zebrafish were cultured at room temperature, and the zebrafish were incubated in culture medium containing 1-phenyl-2-thiourea (PTU) to inhibit melanin production and keep the zebrafish in a transparent state for morphologic observation and fluorescence imaging. QDs at 100 μg/mL−1 were added to confocal dishes containing zebrafish larvae and eggs, and after incubation for 30 min, the culture medium containing QDs was removed, and zebrafish larvae and eggs were fixed using fixative solution, followed by fluorescence imaging of zebrafish using a confocal microscope.

We used various means to characterize the prepared AIS QDs, including TEM and HR-TEM electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR).

The TEM electron microscopy image (Fig. 1 a) shows that the QDs have good dispersion, and the morphology presents a uniformly distributed spherical morphology, and a clear distribution of lattice stripes can be observed from the HR-TEM high-resolution image (Fig. 1b), with good crystallinity and a lattice spacing of about 0.23 nm. we measured the particle sizes of the 80 QDs particles to get the particle-size distribution map (Fig. 1c), and the average size of the particles was is about 3.17 nm, and the particle size distribution ranges from 2.34 nm to 3.71 nm.

The XRD pattern is shown in Fig. 1d, the synthesized QDs have three main peaks at values of 29.45°, 43.17°, and 51.62°, which correspond to the (112), (204), and (312) lattice planes of the tetragonal crystal structure (JCPDS 00–025-1330) [40], and the surface of the prepared material is an orthorhombic-phase structure of the AIS quantum dots.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to analyze the chemical bonds and functional groups on the surface of AIS quantum dots. As shown in the figure (Fig. 1e), the peak at 3374 cm−1 comes from the stretching vibration of O–H bond, the peak at 1606 cm−1 is the result of the action of the stretching vibration of N–H bond and the asymmetric vibration of C = O bond, and additionally the peak at 1385 cm−1 is attributed to the symmetric vibration of COO–.The two peaks at 2989 cm−1 of TGA and 3011 cm−1 of EGTA are the result of the C- H bond stretching vibration, and the peak at 2500 cm−1 on the surface of TGA with the S-H bond disappeared in the pattern of AIS QDs, which was attributed to the deprotonation of the S-H bond, indicating that the ligand formed a metal-sulfur bond on the surface of the quantum dots, which proved the successful encapsulation of the modified ligand on the surface of the quantum dots [41], and the presence of a large number of carboxyl, hydroxyl and other hydrophilic groups on the surface of the AIS QDs, which gave the quantum dots an excellent Water solubility.

The XPS spectra of the AIS QDs are shown in Fig. 1f, which shows the presence of Ag, In, S, and C elements in the samples and a portion of O elements in the AIS quantum dots, which suggests that the C and O elements in the QDs are derived from the EGTA with the TGA, while a portion of them is also derived from air. Two peak positions centered at 373.6 eV and 367.7 eV appear in the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ag 3d (Fig. S1a) and are attributed to Ag 3d2/3 and Ag 3d5/3, respectively. The two peaks at 452.2 eV and 444.6 eV in Fig. S1b are attributed to In 3d3/2 and In 3d5/2, respectively. The peak positions at 164 eV and peaks at 161.6 eV are in Fig. S1c attributed to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, respectively [42].The high-resolution XPS mapping of elemental C is shown in Fig. S1d, in which the three central peaks appearing at 288.32 eV, 286.39 eV, and 284.81 eV correspond to the C = O, C-N, and C–C/C = C bonds, respectively.

We first measured the fluorescence quantum dot yield (PLQY) of AIS quantum dots to be 23.99 % using an FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrometer（Fig. S2 a）.After that we measured the optical properties of the AIS QDs using a spectrophotometer., and from the fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 2a), it can be observed that the optimal emission peak of the AIS QDs is located at 680 nm, and the optimal excitation peak is located at 470 nm, and in the UV–visible absorption spectra, the QDs have a certain absorption capacity in the UV band, however the spectra do not show obvious absorption peaks, and these properties are consistent with the typical characteristic behavior of ternary semiconductor quantum dots [43]. We measured the emission spectra of the QDs under different excitation light (Fig. 2b), and the results show that our synthesized quantum dots have the property of non-excitation-dependent fluorescence, and the positions of the fluorescence emission peaks do not change with the excitation wavelengths, but only the intensity changes. The total fluorescence spectrum of AIS QDs was measured (Fig. 2c) to further investigate its optical properties and confirm the existence of non-excitation-dependent fluorescence. Finally, the near-infrared fluorescence properties of the synthesized QDs were also supported by the coordinates (0.5876, 0.4089) in the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 2d).

The pristine solution of AIS QDs is shown in Fig. 3 a, with an overall orange-red color, and under the irradiation of blue light (450 nm), the quantum dot solution exhibits bright red fluorescence. In addition, we successfully realized the tunable fluorescence for AIS QDs from the orange light band to the near-infrared light band (620 nm-700 nm) by changing the molar ratio of Ag/In（Fig. 3b), the fluorescence emission peak of the quantum dot solution is at 620 nm when the molar ratio of Ag/In is 1:8, and the fluorescence undergoes a red-shift with the gradual increase of the ratio of Ag/In, and the fluorescence is red-shifted When the ratio reaches 1:1, the fluorescence emission peak has reached the near-infrared band (700 nm), which is in line with the typical quantum size effect and quantum domain-limiting effect [44], and we are able to realize tunable biofluorescence imaging through this property in practical applications.

A variety of interfering factors exist in the calcium ion detection process, including pH, temperature, and time will have an impact on the detection experiment, so we first tested the fluorescence intensity change of the AIS QDs solution with and without Ca2+ under different pH conditions, and the results are shown in the graph (Fig. S3a), the fluorescence intensity is more stable under weakly alkaline conditions, and the best effect occurs at pH = 7, which It is in line with the pH range of human body fluids (7.35–––7.45), while a significant decrease occurs under acidic conditions, which is due to the fact that when the pH is low, the higher concentration of hydrogen ions in the QDs solution competes for the ligand site with Ca2+, thus reducing the chelating ability of calcium ions. When the pH is high, the concentration of hydrogen ions in the solution is low and the chelating ability of calcium ions is enhanced, which is also one of the important factors for the regulatory role of calcium ions in organisms. The effect of temperature on the detection of calcium ions is shown in Fig. S3b, the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly at 10–30 °C, and it gradually decreased and stabilized at 40–70 °C. The trend of the effect of time on the fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. S3c, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases in the first fifteen minutes after the addition of Ca2+, and then gradually stabilizes with time. Meanwhile, we also tested the long-time storage ability of the quantum dots, and the results are shown in Fig. S3d, the AIS QDs can still maintain 92 % of the original fluorescence intensity after 45 days of storage at room temperature and under the condition of avoiding light, which possesses the excellent stability and long-lasting storage ability.

We tested the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs versus Ca2+ concentration by fixing a quantum dot solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL−1 as the detection solution, and adding different concentrations of Ca2+ to keep the final concentration range of the quantum dot solution from 0 to 250 μM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of the AIS QDs decreases with the increase of Ca2+ concentration. The fluorescence quenching efficiency Lg (F0/F) had a good linear relationship in the Ca2+ concentration range of 0 ∼ 250 μM, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of Ca2+ in the QDs solution, and F denotes the fluorescence intensity in the presence of Ca2+ in the QDs, as shown in Fig. 4b. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.67 μM using Eq. (2), and the linear regression equation was lg(F0/F) = 0.00216x-0.03567, with a correlation coefficient of R2 of 0.98182. The results showed that our developed probe has good sensitivity and low detection limit for calcium ions.
L
O
D
=
3
σ
S

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of AIS QDs for Ca2+
ce:section.ce:para: One of the important factors affecting the detection of Ca2+ by fluorescent probes when interfering ions. We performed ion interference tests using a variety of common ions including (Mn2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, I-, Na+, HCO3 –, SO3 2-, NO3 2–, SO4 2-). AIS QDs at a concentration of 1 mg/ml was used as the standard assay solution, and 200 μM Ca2+ and other ions were added separately to record the changes in fluorescence intensity. The detection performance of AIS QDs in the simultaneous presence of Ca2+ and other ions was also investigated by competition experiments. The results are shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity decreased by about 60 % after the addition of calcium ions.And the fluorescence intensity of the QDs solution containing Ca2+(Fig. S5, left) and the original QDs solution（Fig. S5, right) under blue light (450 nm) excitation irradiation was able to observe a significant fluorescence reduction phenomenon, indicating that EGTA has a strong chelating ability for calcium ions, while Cu2+ and Fe3+ also showed a relatively obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon, There is still a great deal of disagreement as to the mechanism of its quenching, there are dynamic electron transfer caused by the quenching and static complex aggregation caused by the fluorescence quenching and so on [45,46],However, in practical organism detection and cell imaging applications, Cu2+ and Fe3+ do not have a significant effect due to their much lower content than Ca2+. The coexistence of Ca2+ with interfering ions is also observed, and other ions do not significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of the quantum dots when detecting Ca2+ [47], which further confirms that the probe has the capability of rapid response detection of Ca2+ in complex environments.

We tested the toxicity of AIS QDs using the WST-1 cytotoxicity assay kit as a basis for exploring the in vivo Ca2+ detection and bioimaging capabilities of the QDs.The cell viability of AIS QDs after 24 h of co-incubation with HeLa cells is shown in Fig. S6.After co-incubation with 0–100 μg/mL−1 (5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100 μg/mL−1) QDs, the survival rate of HeLa cells was still higher than 92 %, which indicated that the prepared AIS QDs possessed low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility, and had the potential for bioimaging and biomedical applications.

For the cell imaging experiments, we first used a Leica TCS-SP5 laser confocal microscope for fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells Fig. 5 a-c with Ca2+, d-f without Ca2+), and co-incubated the HeLa cells with AIS QDs (100 μg/mL−1) in a confocal dish for 12 h. After that, the cells were further co-incubated with 500 μM Ca2+ for 6 h. The red fluorescence produced a significant quenching. These findings suggest that AIS QDs are able to penetrate cell membranes to enter cells and have the potential to quantitatively analyze Ca2+ concentrations in cells.

It is well known that the complex behavior of the brain is based on the dynamic activity of neurons and their complex interconnections, and that calcium imaging techniques have the role of monitoring neuronal activity [48,49]. In order to verify whether our prepared quantum dots have the potential to be applied in neural activity detection, we chose the GL261 cell line for our experiments. GL261 cells are neuroglia, a class of non-neuronal cells in the nervous system, which are mainly responsible for providing support and protection to neurons, and are involved in neuronal metabolism and signaling. The results are shown in Fig. 6 a-f. Compared with HeLa cells, GL261 cells are larger in size and rich in cytoplasm, which can also obtain better fluorescence effect, and can also do real-time monitoring of Ca2+ concentration changes, the results show the potential for neuronal detection and imaging applications.

In order to explore more potential capabilities of AIS QDs for biological applications, we chose zebrafish larvae for fluorescence imaging. Zebrafish is a freshwater fish with an adult body length of about 2.5 cm, and it has more than 85 % homology with human genes, so it has been widely used as an experimental fish species for biological research. Fig. 7 a-i show the results of our imaging using zebrafish eggs and larvae (zebrafish treated with melanin inhibitors, showing full-body transparency), where Fig. 7 a-c show the imaging results after 30 min of treatment with AIS QDs, Fig. 7 d-f show the imaging results after co-incubation of AIS QDs with 500 μM Ca2+, and Fig. 7 g-i show the development of the incubation solution containing AIS QDs under the fluorescence images of zebrafish eggs. The results showed that AIS QDs could enter zebrafish within a short period of time and the low cytotoxicity also ensured the normal development of the eggs, indicating that AIS QDs have the potential to be applied to live animal imaging.

We co-incubated HeLa cells using 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and AIS QDs. DAPI is a well-recognized fluorescent dye for targeting the nucleus, and we used it to comparatively analyze the specific distribution of the QDs materials after entering the cells. The results showed that HeLa cells treated with the two fluorescent materials exhibited bright red and blue fluorescence in the cytoplasm and nucleus, respectively (Fig. 8 b and Fig. 8 c), and the composite image (Fig. 8 a) and bright-field image (Fig. 8 d) demonstrated that the stained areas of AIS QDs did not overlap with the stained areas of DAPI, and the analysis of the confocal images using ImageJ software also yielded the results of The confocal images were also analyzed by ImageJ software, and it was concluded that there was no obvious correlation between DAPI and AIS QDs channels. Therefore, it can be inferred that the main distribution range of AIS QDs into the cell after passing through the cell membrane was in the cytoplasm and lysosomes.

In order to analyze and study the mechanism of Ca2+ induced fluorescence quenching of QDs, we conducted several experiments to analyze and verify. First, we measured the change in PLQY of AIS QDs before and after the addition of Ca2+, and the results are shown in Fig. S2, where the fluorescence quantum yield of QDs decreased from 23.99 % to 14.75 % under the effect of 200 μM Ca2+, showing a very obvious decreasing trend.

Fluorescence quenching is usually divided into dynamic and static quenching two pathways, through the observation of the quenching phenomenon, the temperature effect and the absorption spectrum change can determine the quenching form [50].we tested the fluorescence lifetime changes of AIS QDs before and after the addition of calcium ions, as shown in Fig. S7a, which was analytically fitted to the fluorescence lifetimes using the second-order biexponential lifetime decay function, and the red line indicates the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of AIS QDs, whose decay components are 188.09 ns (44.58 %) and 590.47 ns (55.42 %), respectively, with the average lifetimes of about is 411.11 ns (Fig. S8a). The blackline corresponds to the fluorescence lifetime decay curve of AIS + Ca2+ with two components of 114.52 ns (23.28 %) and 433.76 ns (76.72 %), respectively, with an average lifetime of about 359.44 ns (Fig. S8b), fluorescence lifetime shortened with the addition of Ca2+, consistent with the dynamic quenching model. in the above temperature effect experiment has shown that the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs will be increased with the temperature and produce an obvious trend of decreasing, the increase in temperature led to the intensification of the dynamic intermolecular collision, which caused the fluorescence quenching, and secondly, by analyzing the absorption spectra of AIS QDs before and after the addition of Ca2+ (Fig. S7b), it can be seen that there is no obvious change in the absorption spectra, indicating that the quenching occurs in the excited state. In summary, it is possible to judge that the fluorescence quenching of QDs induced by Ca2+ is a dynamic quenching process.

Subsequently, we analyzed the effect of Ca2+ on the zeta potential and particle size of QDs, firstly, the change of zeta potential (Fig. S7c), with the increase of Ca2+ concentration (0–300 μM), the potential increased from −28.2 mV to −11.1 mV, and then saturated and did not increase, which indicated that there was a charge transfer, and similarly, the change of the particle size was analyzed (Fig. S7d). It can be observed that with the increase of Ca2+ concentration, the particle size of AIS QDs increased from 3.53 nm to 170.76 nm, which is due to the fact that EGTA is a kind of chelating agent containing carboxylic acid group, and its carboxylic acid group can form a stable chelate with calcium ions to cause an increase in the particle size, and at the same time, the adsorption of positively charged Ca2+ by the negatively charged QDs solution caused an increase in the electric potential. In summary, the quenching mechanism of this system is consistent with the process of photoinduced electron transfer (PET effect) in the dynamic quenching process [51,52].

In summary, our study successfully synthesized near-infrared fluorescent AIS QDs using EGTA and TGA as a two-ligand system in a one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesized AIS QDs exhibited excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, and stability. Furthermore, they demonstrated a certain fluorescence detection ability for Ca2+, with the detection system showing high selectivity, good anti-interference capability, and rapid response. Additionally, the quantum dots displayed strong biofluorescence imaging capabilities, with noticeable Ca2+-induced changes in fluorescence quenching observable through imaging. These findings highlight the potential application value of the synthesized AIS QDs for real-time rapid detection and bioimaging of Ca2+, offering new avenues for the utilization of semiconductor quantum dots in biochemical analysis.

Jie Wu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jinhua Li: Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mingming Cheng: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Li Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ruhong Yan: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Juan Yue: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFC2403500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82172077, 22201298 and 62027825), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20231207), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.2023SYHZ0037), the Projects of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20200038 and YJKYYQ20210032), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124859.

ce:section.ce:para:
